html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: left: 4x4 square grid (16 cells), all grid lines axis-aligned, equally spaced, drawn as thin black lines

right: same 4x4 grid, transformed by a visibly projective homography—vertical and horizontal grid lines are straight but no longer parallel/equal length, some converge/slant, producing clear perspective skew
arrow between the two grids, indicating the transformation direction left→right
subtle flat grey background planes (one for left, slightly slanted for right) to anchor each grid visually

interactions.label: Homography
interactions.teaches: Controls interpolation between the original regular grid (no transformation) and the fully projectively warped grid, revealing how grid geometry is altered by the homography.
Dragging any corner of the right grid interactively defines a new homography, letting you see how distorting a single corner warps the pattern, creates vanishing points, and destroys parallelism.

demo_steps.title: Regular Grids, No Warp
demo_steps.narration: Both grids start as identical and regular: all cells are perfect squares, and grid lines are parallel and evenly spaced. This is the untransformed reference state.

demo_steps.title: Initiate Projective Warping
demo_steps.narration: As the homography slider increases, the right grid begins to skew. Notice how vertical lines now lean and converge, while the left grid stays unchanged. Some right grid lines are no longer parallel and cell sizes change, but all lines remain perfectly straight.

demo_steps.title: Full Homography: Visible Distortion
demo_steps.narration: With the homography at maximum, the right grid mimics strong perspective: rightmost verticals tilt toward each other and horizontal lines shrink with height. Squares become general quadrilaterals. Despite this distortion, three colinear points stay colinear—a key property of homographies.

demo_steps.title: Manual Warp: Drag a Corner
demo_steps.narration: Try dragging a corner of the right grid. You'll see the warp update in real-time. Skewing a single corner dramatically alters angles and cell shapes, and reveals how parallelism and scale change under a general homography.

plan.camera_suggestion: Front-parallel (orthographic) view with both grids nearly flat; right grid slanted by ~25° azimuth and ~14° elevation to accentuate perspective. Both grids take up similar space and are tightly cropped to the figure's content as in the source, centered horizontally.

camera_view.view_notes: From the source image: left grid is flat (front-on); right grid appears slanted and projectively foreshortened, with right verticals visually converging. Arrow and grids are tightly framed with minimal margin.

inline_constraints.default_view: left grid flat; right grid fully warped to match the original right-panel skew; overall figure silhouette, arrow size, label scale, and crop must match source image
inline_constraints.visible_ui: only one compact slider, centered below or between grids; no filled panel, never covers any grid or arrow
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips appear for slider ('Increase/distort homography') and for draggable corners ('Drag to change homography'); no fixed legends
inline_constraints.framing: match the original's tight horizontal layout and crop—do not expand to fill margins
inline_constraints.camera: match source camera: no dramatic tilt, grids placed to preserve left-right symmetry, and right grid's projective skew rendered identically to the source

plan.notes: Render all grid lines as antialiased thin black THREE.Line geometry; never fill grid faces. Animate all grid vertices using linear interpolation between base grid positions and transformed/warped (projectively mapped) vertices. For drag interaction, update right grid's homography matrix in real time as a user drags any of its four corners (display active corner as a subtle highlight while dragging). Maintain colinearity and line straightness but not length or angle. Arrow should remain prominent and separate. Avoid any panel or overlay that covers grid or arrow. Camera and crop must preserve silhouette, not default to Three.js auto-fit.

plan.contextChunk:
can use the equation of a line in homogeneous coordinates,
$\boldsymbol{l}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{p}=0$. From equation
(@eq-homography_generic), we have that
$\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{H}^{-1} \mathbf{p}'$. Replacing the last equality
into the equation of the line gives
$(\mathbf{H} ^{-\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{l})^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{p'}=0$,
which corresponds to the equation of the line for the projected points
$\mathbf{p}'$.

In a general homography, angles and lengths are not preserved.
Therefore, parallel lines might not remain parallel, and lines of
identical length might have different lengths after a homography.
@fig-illustration_homography2 shows how a planar grid gets transformed
after applying a homography.

![Homography. Colinear points remain colinear after transformation. Angles between lines are not preserved.](figures/homography/example_homography.png){width="70%" #fig-illustration_homography2}

Let's start discussing in which scenarios the coordinates of points
between to images are related by a homography.

### Camera Rotation

Consider the following three images shown in @fig-example_three_images.

:::{#fig-example_three_images layout-ncol="3"}
![](figures/homography/3_IMG_7883_small.jpg){width="52%"}

![](figures/homography/2_IMG_7884_small.jpg){width="30%"}

![](figures/homography/1_IMG_7885_small.jpg){width="30%"}

elements: left: flat square grid of 16 unit squares, 4x4, all lines axis-aligned and equally spaced
right: same 4x4 grid, transformed by a homography — grid appears slanted with lines curved by perspective and edges no longer parallel
arrow between panels indicating transformation direction
flat plane for left grid and slanted plane for right grid

interactions.label: Homography
interactions.teaches: Dragging this slider animates the planar grid smoothly from its original configuration toward the full homography, illustrating how shapes and lines are distorted by a projective transformation.
Dragging any corner of the right-side grid lets you interactively choose a homography, updating the transformation and showing how the geometry changes.

demo_steps.title: Original Regular Grid
demo_steps.narration: This is a flat, regular grid: all squares are equal, lines are parallel and evenly spaced. Nothing has been transformed yet — this is our reference pattern.

demo_steps.title: Beginning the Transformation
demo_steps.narration: As we start increasing the homography slider, the grid begins to warp: the originally parallel lines start converging and the squares distort into general quadrilaterals — but all points that were colinear remain colinear.

demo_steps.title: Full Homography Applied
demo_steps.narration: With the transformation fully applied, the grid shows strong perspective distortion: some lines are no longer parallel, and the sizes of the cells change throughout the grid. Yet the lines themselves remain straight, and colinearity is preserved — a key property of projective geometry.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Front-orthographic view, matching the flat-on orientation of the left grid and the slanted perspective of the right grid; both grids fit comfortably side by side within the crop, with equal visual weight.

camera_view.view_notes: Original left grid is viewed straight-on; right grid is skewed to show planar distortion. Aspect ratio matches figure: both grids centered, arrow between panels remains clear.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the source: left panel flat grid, right panel skewed grid; spacing, sizing, and margins retained
inline_constraints.visible_ui: single compact slider at lower edge (between panels) for 'Homography'; no filled panel and no occlusion of arrow or important lines
inline_constraints.explainers: tooltips pop up on hover for 'Homography' slider and for any point dragged on right grid
inline_constraints.framing: original grid placement, arrow position, whitespace/margins all preserved; scene content must not force-fill the iframe
inline_constraints.camera: azimuth, elevation, and crop precisely chosen to match silhouette and arrangement of both grids+arrow

interactionPlan.notes: Animate grid vertices from flat to homography using vertex lerp from identity (left) to projective transform (right). For direct manipulation, update the homography in real-time as any corner of the right grid is dragged in the plane, smoothly affecting the entire grid. Arrow should remain static and visible, not animated. Use line geometry for grid lines; do not fill grid faces. Three.js rendering: antialiased thin black lines, no shading, white background.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The right grid in the rendered output has visible circular corner handles that do not appear in the source figure.
Some grid lines in the right panel are not perfectly aligned to match the perspective of the reference image (possibly due to editable corners or handle placement).
The UI slider is visible at the bottom left, while the reference image has no controls or text.
Arrow proportions and position are slightly different (arrowhead smaller and the arrow is less centered in the screenshot than in the reference).
There is a slight difference in crop and whitespace: the generated figure is more zoomed in and less centered within its panel.
evaluation.notes: The interactive homography and grid are correct in concept with useful guided/drag controls, but visual fidelity is reduced by unwanted handles, slightly off grid proportions, and more zoomed/cropped appearance than the minimalist source.
action_items: Hide the draggable circle handles by default for inline/first frame rendering to match the clean, handle-free source figure.
Adjust camera zoom and object centering so that grid panels match the frame, whitespace, and aspect ratio of the source image closely.
Tweak the arrow size and position to better match the reference for both shaft thickness and arrowhead scale.
Ensure that the initial grid perspective matches the exact angles of the reference (adjust default homography values).

feedback.rationale: The failure modes and action items identify concrete execution details—unwanted handles, zoom/crop, grid alignment, and arrow style—that are fixable via generator improvements rather than changes to the conceptual plan.
action_items: Hide the draggable circle handles by default for inline/first frame rendering to match the clean, handle-free source figure.
Adjust camera zoom and object centering so that grid panels match the frame, whitespace, and aspect ratio of the source image closely.
Tweak the arrow size and position to better match the reference for both shaft thickness and arrowhead scale.
Ensure that the initial grid perspective matches the exact angles of the reference (adjust default homography values).
